commentBody: This was, like, quite amusing. Thanks, Rebecca and Christina. It's fine with me to have a fun Sunday that doesn't put up much of a fight (although I really wished Saturday had). I'm glad the constructors went with the themers they did; I hope FOUGHT, LIKE, THE DEVIL?, which they discarded, does not describe anyone's solve today.
Totally.
16:38, and a 1619-day streak that must end because tomorrow brings a no-phones, six day rafting trip in Idaho. Farewell, fond streak!

I won’t be the last to say there was a lot to LIKE here. This was a cute theme and they got better as the puzzle progressed. Always happy to see a byline by Rebecca or Christina, what a great team!

A lot of Gods and two Hawaiian islands (LANAI or KAUAI, the age old question, along with SCARF or SNARF?) My favorite clue: “They shine during the day”. Terrific grid!

Like, that was fun.  Seven very common phrases, yet each is a debut. Totally rad.  

I always enjoys puzzles from Rebecca and Christina. Nicely done, ladies. 

Tended garden with a brown thumb, in Valleyspeak?

GREW LIKE A WEED
This was so breezy, I felt as if this was a Monday puzzle.

Just in case Marshall (or anyone!) doesn't read my response to his post, or it doesn't get posted at all, let me remind people that if you are on a streak and have to take a break, if you pick up where you left off when you get back, your streak will continue. If you do the puzzle for the day you get back, and then go back and do the ones you missed, your streak will start over at one. Fingers crossed!

I loved it; the theme entries had me like, laughing out loud!

I suppose it was on the easy side, since it has been my fastest Sunday yet, but I don't mind.
Way too easy, even for Sunday.. 11 minutes..

Try again, emus:

Report that Judith from “Who’s the Boss” was g@γ?

OUT, LIKE, A LIGHT.
Valley speak has a lot more to LIKE about it than just that one word. I mean, GAG ME WITH A SPOON, surely you can think of more TOTALLY TUBULAR clues. Actually, I really enjoyed this puzzle.
I, like, appreciated the premise but solving it was, like, too easy.
I thought this was very clever and I, for one, knew none of the trivia so it was not easy. Great puzzle.
I think this was my fastest ever clean solve for a Sunday. I have faster times that were glitched. 19 minutes
It’s giving crossword

Did I, like, do that!? 

Why yes, fer sure!  

It was so totally rad, I'm gonna get my AQUAnet from my LAIR and tease my hair into Liberty spikes—as DIOSMIO intended! 

How very.
Kudos to Christina and Rebecca for the puzzle and to Caitlin for the column, the headline, and most especially for the photo with all the AQUAS and its clever caption.

This puzzle was so corny I couldn’t not, like, like it.

And a tip of the hat to Madame Brendlar who introduced me to Le Petit Prince in high school French.
Oops. Just realized I threw an extra not in there.
What an entertaining puzzle! I loved it!
I already know this puzzle is gonna make solvers wrestle house-pets, in Valleyspeak (FIGHT LIKE CATS AND DOGS).

Liked this puzzle a lot! Lots of clues were at a high level and felt fresh. The cliché phrases viewed in a new light were plenty of fun. And I appreciated a large quantity of interesting general knowledge beyond pop culture. It was just fun to solve this puzzle.

If I'd do one thing differently, it might be to avoid repeating the word LIKE over and over but instead maybe position it as a mystery word (perhaps indicated by one circled square in each theme answer) that it is up to the solver to determine.

98A MEN--Perfect for Father's Day God bless Mother Nature, she's a single woman too She took off to heaven and she did what she had to do She taught every angel and rearranged the sky So that each and every woman could find her perfect guy It's raining men! Hallelujah! It's raining men! Amen! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l5aZJBLAu1E
Took me a while to figure out the top left corner, but got it eventually. :)
I must be dreaming! An actual NYT Sunday crossword puzzle! I was beginning to doubt there were constructors who could do that anymore. Thankfully, there are at least two. Greatly appreciated.
Loved this theme! So clever and funny
This was a wonderful puzzle. A classic Sunday crossword. Thank you Rebecca and Christina.
Managed in the end but got myself stuck a few times: water colors (blues), pretty fishy (koi) etc.

(First attempt got emu'ed...) I loved this! Nothing but an outstanding puzzle could come from a mind meld of Rebecca and Christina, powerhouses on their own, and a TIDAL force to be reckoned with together. I started at the bottom of the grid, so figured out WORKED LIKE A CHARM quickly, setting a fun tone for all the themers. Then I had a blast with the fill which took me to many places... . BABEL - great movie . ANTOINE de Saint-Exupéry - earlier today I came across a quote of his, simple in wording, but nailing the concept of perception: "The meaning of things lies not in the things themselves, but in our attitude toward them." (from "Wisdom of the Sands") . YSL - that other "Saint," Monsieur Yves Saint Laurent, for whose cosmetics company I had the honor of working while he was still alive and had a say, insisting on quality. Yes, he was responsible for the first Beatnik collection (which was also the first prêt-à-porter at a time of only couture on runways.) He put women in stylized motorcycle leather jackets and put models in thigh-high boots. He put women in pant suits. He put Black models on the runway. And he put art into clothing, giving us the Mondrian dress. You could say I worship his creativity and genius, as I do Rebecca's and Christina's. I rode like the wind on the wings of this amazing puzzle and wanted more. Thank you to both! https://youtu.be/kA9uaBqvRtA?is=4Gkb8jyvziuwUIG2

Was about to look up the Mathematician with the Nobel prize in economics when my brain like clicked in on A Beautiful Mind ending and John NASH receiving the Nobel prize. Got hung up in the top right corner but enjoyed the solve.

Beyond CLEVER puzzle and it was good to practice my very scratchy vocal fry and upvoice?  These two constructors, separately or together, are always a joy to solve. They ACED it.

Maybe I just hung out with the wrong people, but I never really heard Valley Speak practiced unironically except on TV. I mean, the "like" thing is now so prevalent as a filler word or disfluency, so true Valley Speak would need not only a plethora of "likes" but also vocal fry, upvoice, and unaware use of some of the main words and phrases, including grody to the max, gag me with a spoon, bogus, etc. Was there ever such a culture, or was it all made up by TV for sitcoms and primetime shows, and then adopted and parroted by the viewing public, not vice versa?
In New England vernacular, this was wicked good. Yessuh.
Caught on to the theme early but the bottom fill was tricky enough that I didn't blast through the whole puzzle. Took me a pleasant 16:10 to get the Gold Star

For a more academic discussion of the use of “like” here’s an article by my favorite linguist (actually, one of my favorite academicians, period), John McWhorter: https://www.nytimes.com/2024/02/15/opinion/casual-speech-like.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share
Whoo! That went fast!

I liked the humorous theme today.  Maybe leaned on a stereotype a bit much, but satisfyingly humorous just putting LIKE in the middle of phrases.

I'm a little annoyed that with IHOP in the clue it was SOLD LIKE HOTCAKES and not SOLD LIKE PANCAKES.  That made that area of the puzzle hard to get because pancake is literally in the acronym.

Good cluing overall.
One of my favorites ever!

OMG! They closed the GAP at the mall!
My life is ov- wait a minute, is that our song playing on your Walkman?

I SHOOKLIKEALEAF,  ain't ASKIN for disgrace
WATCHEDLIKEAHAWK, in case I ERRED and needed to backspace

Let's spell to-tally like they do!
You know they're CLEVER and we're getting better (it's ABOUTIME)
There's nothing Becs and Chris can't do
No valley here, just ALTITUDES!

Dreamt of better grids, and WEDO think they ACED!
Trapped by ANEMIC clues, they JILT that clichéd paste
They made a puzzle grid to test this Sunday race 
KNEEDEEP in OATHS some streaks had long gone by

GOODLOSER cries INSIDE
With brain heavylikeOXIDE

Let's spell the words just like these two!
ILLBET you solved it,  and you ITCH to LAUD them, all the time!
There's nothing Becks and Chris can't do
No valley here, just ALTITUDES!


OKDONE. Can we stop for some Orange Julius?

What it lacked in difficulty it made up for in smiles and head shakes.

I squeaked with anticipation when I saw the constructor names  and the synergy of their collaboration did not disappoint.

An unusual Sunday to be driven so overwhelmingly by charm.
Too easy for Sunday. Theme immediately obvious, theme clues easy to guess.
I'm no cannibal, but this puzzle had me cooking like a chef.
OMG, I am like so sure! Me class of 1982, Southern California, Frank Zappa,..We were vacuous airheads, and we liked it! Thank you, Rebecca and Christina!
My first Sunday that I finished without lookups! Even though it was certainly easier than normal, it still feels like an accomplishment. Such a, like, fun puzzle!

I'll tell you one thing for sure--you did *not* want SOLDLIKEpanCAKES for 24A.

'Cause if you did, you're as bad as I am.

Yet another use of the word LIKE that is ultra-common in my experience at least for 40+ years is for the phrase "to be like" used in the present tense to mean "said". As in "I'm like "What are  you doing here?' and he's like 'I dunno, nothin' really.'"

I'm not sure if that's part of Valleyspeak or not.
Spent a delightful evening at a members event at the Charles Schulz Museum in Santa ROSA, CA, only to come home and find it in the crossword. I love synchronicity

I'm a bit embarrassed to say that even though I claim to love math, I had never heard of John Nash until "A Beautiful Mind" came out.

Even more embarrassing, at no point in that movie did anything I recognize as "math" appear.  The notation, the symbols, nothing at all made the slightest sense.

And I know it's not the movie just doing crazy stuff.  It's like there's a giant piece of math out there I'm completely unaware of.

If only we had information like that at our fingertips...

Like how cool was that? Alicia Silverstone had us all enamored  with Valleyspeak. Whatever! I’m going on a 90’s spew at this moment.

The world would go around me
I ain’t the sharpest tool in the shed
She was looking kinda dumb
With her finger and her thumb
In the shape of an L on her forehead!

Oh, thank you for this wonderful puzzle. It is like the literal best!
OMG, that was, like, totally awesome

Yeah, nice but pretty easy. And I say that not just as a former Californian. (15:14!)
That was like totally fun. Quick for a Sunday (my third new PB this week, alongside Thursday and Saturday), but delightful!
Fun puzzle!

No one could have been entirely clueless about the Valley Girl talk. Amy Heckerling, who wrote and directed the brilliant take-off on Jane Austen's "Emma," flooded the language with Valley Girl tropes, much of it harvested from what she heard and some of it she just invented. If you haven't seen the movie, you've missed the source of some very funny dialog that energized teen language everywhere. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xRQPtUBxDmM

I can solve a little quicker if I'm at my PC with a full size keyboard.  But some of y'all are just on another level than this old plodder.  50 minutes on the dot, and several minutes better than my Sunday average.

Nice crossword and theme.

My one nitpick is with the article. Mozart did not originate the tune to Twinkle, Twinkle Little Star. It was a French nursey rhyme "Ah vous dirai-je, Maman" published in 1961 when Mozart was 5. Although that was the age when Mozart started composing, he certainly was not getting published at that age. The tune is thought to have been around for about 20 years before it was published and therefore before Mozart was born.
I mean, OMG, that was, like, totally awesome. Thank you, Rebecca and Christina! I also, like, like the alternative entries that didn’t fit.
Like, totally fun way to finish my Saturday night and start my Sunday morning. Theme answers all made me smile. Worked like a charm for me!
Really, really enjoyable!
Clever and funny, and, like, really enjoyable, thanks so much!

Well. 
I am no doubt the only person here, or in the entire world, never to have heard of Valley Girls or the way they speak. I was quite mystified by the clues.
I did enjoy solving the other clues though, a bit nice wordplay although mostly synonyms.

In 1975 I was sitting on a beach in Santa Barbara and a beautiful blonde boy with a surfboard informed me that 'rad' was the latest slang, and would replace 'far out'.

Excellent puzzle. Fun, creative, just the right difficulty... I can't wait for the next one. 

I'm loving Christina Iverson's puzzles lately. 
I'm going back to the archive and making sure I have had a go at her creations. I'm not a solver who's going to break any speed records, I enjoy the different elements too much to breeze through them. 

Along with her collaborations. They are some of the Best NYT Crosswords ever. So now I've added Rebecca to my list. Great Sunday puzzles really set up some good karma for the upcoming week.

This puzzle was a hoot! Like totally.
Overusing the word LIKE is almost as distracting to me as upspeak, and this puzzle was so timely since I had a phone conversation yesterday with a good friend who happens to live in Southern California - but who is decidedly not a valley girl - who says LIKE many times in every sentence. She pointed it out herself at one point and I rode the wave and started to stop her every time she said it, also explaining that it makes it difficult for me to focus on the actual conversation, but it all felt rude, so I would LIKE love to know how else I might politely help her. Any suggestions?

To my very delicate ears, the repetitive "like" is not nearly as annoying as the "wwwwah" at the end of a word, like "wowwwwah," or as Caitlin stated, ya knooowwwww. But that needs the schwa at the end to make it truly insufferable. I think the use of "like" has now transcended all generations. I even find my pedantic self using it from time to time, maybe as a replacement for "um?"

Something I rarely (okay, never) write on Sundays: Like, I really, like, *liked* this puzzle!

About forty minutes of work, almost all of which I enjoyed. Very few winces, and none of those elicited by the thematic entries or their silly clues. Having two entries that included just "like" rather than "like a" struck me as a nice thematic feature.

And I even learned that "before" can technically be a conjunction, and is, for example in "By this count I shall be much in years / Ere I again behold my Romeo!" 

For me, a Sunday POY contender. Thanks!
At first I was all like, "barf out." But by the end I had to confess this is probably the best theme I've ever encountered.

This was like, so totally like, rad, you know?

I’m like, not *even* with it?

Like tooootally POY? 

I’m like so sad cuz like, 
we have to like paint our fence, and
it’s like, sooooo hot today?

I’m gonna like, get like, sweaty
which sooooo grody?

Like, gag me with roller…

Y’ know?

This, like, super-rad puzzle led me over to Language Log, where there are several old posts about the use of “like,” such as:

itre.cis.upenn.edu/~myl/languagelog/archives/000141.html

Whew. and Wow! Not an easy one for me, of course, but a really clever theme and just had a great time figuring out the theme answers once I caught on (needed lots of crosses, of course). And... puzzle find: A Sunday from August 12, 2012 by Patrick Berry with the title: "The Meaning of It." Some theme clues and and answers: "Talking isn't going to reseal that wine bottle!" PUTACORKINIT "I already know my homemade cold cream is useless!" DONTRUBITIN "Of course this car isn't voice-controlled." ITGOESWITHOUTSAYING "This tippy Christmas tree is driving me crazy!" ICANTSTANDIT And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/12/2012&h=38a See you later. ....
This was totally tubular. Seriously I am having such a bad week and me and my husband were laughing out loud on this one! Really great puzzle. Thank you thank you!
Hilarious! I couldn’t help myself from reading aloud each theme answer in a nasal Valley voice. Like, totally awesome!
I like, loved it!

An uncannily fun puzzle!

Santa ROSA was also the home of Myles Callum, a very generous crossword constructor who encouraged my first attempt (filled without software!) and even shared his private wordlists with me. I remember him fondly.

I like both these constructors a lot separately and I was sure I'd like them a lot as a team.  And I did.  If you make a Sunday puzzle playful enough, it will never be a slog regardless of its length and this is very playful.  Growing up in NYC, I may have never heard a Valley Girl in the flesh as it were, but she's been parodied plenty in the past.  Now, if only I could remember the accent -- but I don't.

The overuse of the word" like", however, isn't restricted to the Valley.  It's bled into speaking patterns all around the country and it's incredibly annoying.  I have heard interviews with Really Famous People who, if you took the word "like" away from them, would be rendered mute.

Also -- why do Valleys tend to produce idiosyncratic speaking patterns.  While I've never met a Valley Girl, as an East Coaster, I've met more than a few women -- it's always women! -- with Locust Valley Lockjaw.  This is a pseudo-posh and very pinched way of speaking in which the jaw, lips and teeth barely move at all.  Is there also a Napa Valley way of speaking?   A Death Valley way?  Just wondering.

As for the puzzle, it's a delightful theme idea that's very amusingly executed.  I found it fun from beginning to end.
Anybody else surprised to learn that John TESH won a Nobel prize? Like, wow...
So funny! I saw the byline before I started, so I went in with high expectations and was not disappointed. Laughed out loud at every single theme answer.
What a fun and delightful puzzle! A great start to my day

Trying again:
Started at the bottom with my Hawaiian Islands.  Got BOISE (The Gem State, eh?) and then 121A was the first Themer of the SEVEN! to fall.  "Oh.  THAT Valley."  QED.

I enjoyed this very much, glad to have a faster solve than the Spelling Bee, which required 278 or so just for Genius.  I quit after 314 points, just tired of conjugating!

Argghhhhhh…let my Wordle (500+), Connections, and Strands streaks lapse yesterday. What a dope!

Fortunately though my crossword streak is intact at 2020.

As a person who grew up in the SF Valley, I felt nostalgia from this theme. Yes, we absolutely talked like this and worse. I have old videos as a 12 year old saying “totally” and “for sure” and our parents always tried to break us of the “like” habit. But we persisted. The kids from the valley were so maligned at that time, going to the beach was a risky business. “Vals go home” graffiti was a threat, but we still made the journey. Two bus transfers, many hours of travel and disgust from locals would not defeat us. Have a rad Sunday everyone!
Nice one! Filled it in 22 minutes without clues, but need to find incorrect square(s) for the gold star. I enjoyed the Valley Speak clues - one place where “like” is acceptable.
Nice one! Filled it in 22 minutes without clues, but need to find incorrect square(s) for the gold star. I enjoyed the Valley Speak clues - one place where “like” is acceptable.

I've known about the Large Hadron Collider for many years, but it never occurred to me that it was a particle. Seriously, I thought it was a place name. So TIL it means "fat particle" because it has more than one QUARK. Lepton, by contrast, means "skinny particle."

Delightful trip down memory lane! A perfect Sunday, IMO.

I loved the crossing of the two Hawaiian islands!

One more puzzle find - one of the most unusual I've ever encountered. A Thursday from June 30, 2016 by Johnathan M. Kaye. Hard to describe this one. First... the pair of clues that were the "reveal." 38a. "With 39-across, refill to capacity or a hint to interpreting the clues at 17-, 27-, 46- and 671-across." And those two answers: TOP OFF And the theme clues and answers: "BOB" DEFECTIVEBULLET "TB8L" ADOREDSUPERSTAR "8V8TB" SHAPEDLIKEANEGG "VMB" BRIGHTLYCOLORED Had to ponder for a while before it all finally dawned on me. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/30/2016&h=46a ,,,

And he put art into clothing, giving us the Mondrian dress. You could say I worship his creativity and genius, as I do Rebecca's and Christina's. Well, I did 99% of the puzzle with no lookups but finally relented and used autocheck to get SALON and SALADA and SNARF, so sadly, no gold star today.  I'd never heard the word SNARF vs SCARF and had not seen SALON used in that manner.

"It's Raining Men" is a gay anthem? That's presumptuous; there are many more in the gay community who'd have chosen "Over the Rainbow" for such an honor.

but "dropped like flies" doesn't mean to make a mistake... the double meaning throughout the puzzle doesn't work here... my husband and I were sure it was "dropped like balls"... though I guess that has a triple meaning

I confidently wrote SCARF in 18-down and just thought SALOC was a new word to me. Dang.  If not for searching through the whole thing for that mistake, woulda had the thing done in 15 or so.

I am in awe of this clue construction. Looking back over it, there isn't a single thing in the grid I hadn't heard of before except for TERMSHEET, but the clues were so inventive that the whole thing felt fresh. 

I'd love to map out how they spaced their misdirects throughout the grid. Those witty clues became clear only when I came back around. The solve was gentle, but it was delightful.

@Jeb if I recall correctly, the cutoff is the drop time for the second puzzle after the current one - so you have until the Tuesday puzzle drops to complete Sunday and still get a gold star. 

But *do not* open Monday until Sunday is complete, because doing the puzzles out of order will also break a streak.

@Dan

The whole point of the puzzle was "like" how a Valley Girl, (circa 1980s) spoke. They, like, couldn't get a sentence out without, like, putting the word "like" in every, like, sentence. 

Today's kids still do it. My grandson now has that habit and it is no less, like, irritating. It means nothing. It contributes nothing to the meaning of a sentence. It makes me want to pull what little hair I have left out.

All this to say, without repeating the word, the theme wouldn't have worked.

@Marlene
I certainly understand how frustrating it must be to pull on hair that has been left out.  🙂

@SP

A fascinating phenomenon, to me, is how the word "like" has come to serve as the most ubiquitous filler word in other languages, as well.  

Among just the languages in which I'm conversant there's "כאילו" (k'eeloo) in Hebrew, "so" and "wie" in German, "como" in Spanish...

Any linguists out there care to weigh in on whether this is a natural linguistic propensity (i.e. using "like" as an informal substitute for "as if") or the influence of English-speaking culture on world speech?  Or a combination?

@Matt

But I think I'd feel small in any event, because when one faces the fact that our influence over events, any of us, is minuscule compared to cosmic times and distances, it's hard not to feel small.

I would say that it is quite likely that humans as we know them will either become extinct, or go through a population decline that emerges, if it does, almost completely unaware of us, at least initially.

Furthermore, it is very likely no intelligent species anywhere in the universe will be aware we ever existed.

@Nancy
Semi-serious reply: valleys isolate their inhabitants. Isolated populations preserve patterns of speech. (Hence, you could land at a Newfoundland outport until the1980s or so and hear Elizabethan English as Shakespeare would have known it.) So you're more likely to find linguistic curiosities among such populations because they are not erased or smoothed over by social mingling with other accents.

@SBK in TO But residents of the San Fernando Valley mingled with the rest of Southern California all the time. And a significant portion of the San Fernando Valley speaks Spanish primarily. The language depicted in ths puzzle is very time specific. 
The song "Valley Girl" is over 40 years old and definitely doesn't represent how people in the area speak now, which is pretty much the same as all the other parts of Southern California.
